Description: Optimal average power for Ruthenium oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength for efficient Ruthenium oxide absorption with minimal substrate interaction
Beam spot diameter for precise Ruthenium surface cleaning
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Fluence threshold for Ruthenium oxide removal without bulk material damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled Ruthenium oxide ablation
Optimal scanning speed for uniform Ruthenium surface treatment
Recommended number of passes for complete Ruthenium oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform Ruthenium surface cleaning

Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning of reflective metals like Ruthenium; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and heat input to avoid melting (melting point ~2334°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and general metal cleaning guidelines (e.g., SPIE proceedings on laser ablation).

Effective for contamination removal; slower for heavy contamination (100-500 mm/s), faster for light (500-2000 mm/s). Recommended 500 mm/s for Ruthenium to ensure uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation due to its thermal conductivity (~120 W/m·K). Source: Industry standards from Laserax and CleanLaser equipment manuals.

Nanosecond regime preferred for Ruthenium cleaning to ablate contaminants while minimizing heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications. 20 ns recommended for low thermal damage on high-reflectivity surfaces. Source: Laser physics studies (e.g., Applied Surface Science journal on ns vs. ps ablation thresholds for noble metals).

Standard for pulsed cleaning; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat buildup on Ruthenium's reflective surface (reflectivity ~60% at 1064 nm). 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: high freq increases overlap heating. Source: Academic literature (e.g., Optics & Lasers in Engineering on repetition rate effects in metal cleaning).

Fluence range for contaminant ablation without substrate damage; Ruthenium damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to high melting point. Recommended 1 J/cm² for safe cleaning of light contaminants. Source: Ablation threshold studies (e.g., Journal of Applied Physics on noble metal thresholds; conservative estimate for Ru similar to Pt/Rh).

Smaller spots (50-200 μm) for fine surface prep on Ruthenium components; larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform energy distribution on small industrial parts. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers for precision cleaning.

Single pass for light contamination; multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers to avoid damage. Recommended 2 passes for Ruthenium to ensure complete removal while respecting thermal properties. Source: Industry case studies (e.g., laser cleaning of catalytic surfaces in chemical engineering reports).

Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation; 50% optimal for Ruthenium to prevent uneven heating on reflective surfaces. Typical range 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization studies (e.g., Journal of Laser Applications on scan overlap for metal surface treatment).
